frameworks.name: inverse decision theory
frameworks.one_line: Estimate the implicit loss function behind visual memory errors from behavior.

frameworks.name: optimal/efficient memory under capacity constraint
frameworks.one_line: Memory minimizes expected loss subject to a capacity bound.

frameworks.name: additive noise / noise-corrupted encoding models
frameworks.one_line: Standard view that memories are perceptual signals plus noise is rejected.

frameworks.name: gaze correction following saccadic error (natural task)
frameworks.one_line: Used as benchmark task whose loss function predicts memory behavior.

core_question: Can the structure of visual working memory errors be explained by an implicit loss function rather than additive perceptual noise?
key_assumptions: Visual working memory has a fixed capacity constraint.
Errors carry differential costs depending on the task.
An optimally efficient memory minimizes expected loss under that constraint.
manipulations: Delayed recall visual memory experiment varying stimulus features.
Comparison across multiple empirical VWM datasets.
Loss function inferred from behavior vs. predicted from gaze-correction task.
key_findings: An empirical loss function can be recovered from delayed recall data via inverse decision theory.
Inferred loss aligns with the loss expected from a natural saccadic-error correction task.
The loss-function account fits memory data better than additive-noise models across datasets.
interpretations: Visual working memory errors reflect cost-weighted optimization, not just noisy encoding.
Capacity-limited memory is shaped by ecologically relevant task costs.
